video_title: Are Markets Becoming Living Systems? | Systematic Investor | Ep. 402
gist: Richard Brennan argues markets are complex adaptive systems whose behavior is set by their 'agent ecology', and that the 20-year rise of price-insensitive passive capital has diluted the balancing (convergent) agents while leaving the reinforcing (divergent) ones intact, structurally hardening the trend/cascade architecture and making markets more inelastic. He presents agent-based-model evidence that a ~25% minority of price-conditioned 'chartists' triggers a phase transition into the fat-tailed, memory-laden, volatility-clustering fingerprint seen across 68 markets and 40 years.

themes.theme: Passive ownership has restructured the agent ecology

insights.claim: Passive vehicles hold roughly 60% of US equity fund assets, and once benchmark-hugging pensions, sovereign wealth funds and endowments are included, the price-insensitive share of US equity ownership is meaningfully higher than 60%.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: If most ownership no longer reacts to price, the marginal active dollar sets prices in a much thinner pool — a structural reason to expect larger directional moves.
insights.quote: the most commonly cited figure puts passive at around about 60% of US equity fund assets

insights.claim: The single biggest structural change in global markets over two decades is not algos, HFT or mega-cap dominance — those are downstream effects — but the transfer of ownership from price-sensitive to price-insensitive agents.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: Reframes what to monitor: watch the price-insensitive ownership share, not the usual narratives, as the driver of market fragility.
insights.quote: The structural change is the transfer of a growing share of market ownership from price sensitive agents to price insensitive ones.

insights.claim: Passive is price-insensitive by design: it buys index constituents at any price in index proportion, does not buy more when cheap or less when expensive, and therefore performs no price discovery.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: Explains why valuations can stretch indefinitely in index names — there is no built-in seller of the overpriced.
insights.quote: Price is not an input into its decision function.

insights.claim: Passive index funds belong on the reinforcing/divergent side of markets because cap-weighted inflows buy more of whatever is already largest, amplifying the existing distribution rather than fading it.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: Implies passive inflows mechanically entrench winners and concentration, a tailwind for momentum and mega-cap leadership.
insights.quote: Passive joins that divergent side because it's it's flows buys more of what is already large.

insights.claim: Passive does not eliminate balancing agents (value investors, counter-trend traders, market makers still exist) but dilutes their proportional weight, thinning the balancing feedback that used to cap reinforcing loops.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: Trends should run further before meeting resistance — the brake, not the accelerator, is what has weakened.
insights.quote: what has been diluted is the proportional weight of the agents conditioned on price relative to value

insights.claim: The individual-level rationality of owning low-cost index funds and the system-level harm of price-insensitivity are separate questions with different answers; millions of sensible individual choices can change market structure in ways no individual intended.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: Defuses the 'passive is fine' rebuttal — the systemic fragility argument stands even if indexing is correct for the saver.
insights.quote: Index funds can be a sensible choice for the individual and the aggregate consequence of millions of those sensible choices can have changed the structural behavior in markets

themes.theme: Markets as complex adaptive systems, not equilibrium clocks

insights.claim: Markets are complex adaptive systems whose prices, trends and cascades emerge from interactions among agents holding partial models — not a reflection of any underlying economy or fundamental value.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: Shifts the key analytical question from 'what is fair value' to 'who are the agents and how do they interact'.
insights.quote: A market is constituted by the interactions of the population of agents, each holding a partial model of the situation

insights.claim: The market is an autonomous system that merely correlates with — rather than reflects — the economy, the firm and the banking system; fundamental value lives in a separate system that need not agree with the market.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: Justifies ignoring fundamentals as a price anchor and explains persistent valuation overshoots like April's moves.
insights.quote: to say the market should reflect fundamental value is to assume a binding correlation between two autonomous systems that, on evidence, don't always agree

insights.claim: The equilibrium/efficient-markets model treats markets as clocks (additive parts that don't observe each other) when they are flocks (agents that imitate, panic and anchor to each other), making classical finance a category error.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: Models built on equilibrium assumptions systematically underestimate tail risk and trend persistence.
insights.quote: It's treating a flock as if it were a clock

insights.claim: The equilibrium model survived because it is convenient — it produces spreadsheet numbers to value securities and convince investors — not because it is correct; the honest alternative is a hard research program nobody wants.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: Suggests an enduring edge for practitioners who abandon equilibrium framing while institutions stay anchored to it.
insights.quote: the equilibrium model has survived because it's convenient, not because it's right

insights.claim: Emergent market structures — trends, crashes, volatility clustering and concentration in a handful of names — are real and structural, not anomalies or coincidences, like a starling murmuration that self-organizes with no central control.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: Treating trends/cascades as durable features rather than aberrations supports systematically trading them.
insights.quote: trends are not anomalies, cascades are not aberrations, concentration is not coincidence

insights.claim: Microstructure research (Bouchaud, Gabaix, Cohen) finds most price movement cannot be attributed to identifiable news — prices move on no-news days and out of proportion to the news that does arrive.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: Undercuts news-driven trading narratives; the size of a move tells you more about internal positioning than about the catalyst.
insights.quote: The vast majority of price movement can't be attributed to identifiable news.

insights.claim: Most price movement is endogenous — the system processing itself in a loop where a breakout draws momentum buyers, which alters volatility, which triggers vol-targeting and rebalancing, which forces further price adjustment.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: Maps the mechanical chain that sustains cascades — useful for anticipating second-order forced flows after an initial move.
insights.quote: Markets don't simply reflect the world, they process themselves.

insights.claim: News is a trigger, not a driver: the match starts the fire but its shape and extent depend on the dryness of the wood (the sensitized feedback architecture), so a sensitized system can produce arbitrarily large moves from small sparks.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: Position for outsized moves when the system is 'dry' (calm, leveraged, concentrated), regardless of catalyst size.
insights.quote: The fire's shape and extent depends on the dryness of the wood, not the size of the match.

insights.claim: Soros's reflexivity is the operational mechanism of the endogenous engine: price-sensitive agents' reactions reshape the market, which reshapes the next observation and reaction in a continuous recursive loop.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: Explains why trends persist past any catalyst and why mean-reversion models fail in reflexive regimes.
insights.quote: their reactions reshape the markets which reshape the next observation which reshape the next reaction

insights.claim: Market behavior is governed by two feedback modes — reinforcing (momentum, stop-losses, vol-targeting, index inclusion, dealer delta-hedging, passive inflows) that amplifies moves, and balancing (value buyers, overpriced-asset sellers, market makers, counter-trend) that absorbs them.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: A practical taxonomy: gauge which mode dominates to judge whether a market is set to trend, range or break.
insights.quote: When reinforcing loops dominate, the system becomes directional in trends form. When balancing loops dominate, the system becomes range bound.

insights.claim: Divergent traders (trend followers, momentum) ride moves while convergent traders (value, mean-reversion, counter-trend) fade them; passive sits on the divergent/reinforcing side, so the reinforcing camp has grown while the convergent camp has been diluted.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: The net shift toward reinforcing capital is a structural tailwind for trend-following returns.
insights.quote: Reinforcing feedback comes from divergent traders and from passive long only investors.

insights.claim: Phase transitions in volatility occur when influence shifts rapidly between reinforcing and balancing loops — the market 'breathes' between trending and range-bound states.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: Watch for rapid handoffs between the two regimes as the precursor to volatility surges.
insights.quote: When the influence shifts rapidly from one to the other, phase transitions occur and volatility surges.

themes.theme: Market inelasticity and the flow multiplier

insights.claim: Because price-insensitive holders own the bulk of the float, active flow must find counterparties in a structurally thin price-sensitive pool, so each unit of flow moves price more — markets have become structurally less elastic.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: Expect outsized, non-linear price impact from modest active flows — sizing and liquidity assumptions from 20 years ago are now too generous.
insights.quote: fewer price sensitive players in the active mix means more violent price action per unit of flow, not less

insights.claim: Research (Gabaix, Koijen; Brennan also cites Caban/Kogan and Bouchaud) finds an inelasticity multiplier around 5 — $1 of net buying can add roughly $5 to aggregate market cap and $1 of selling destroy ~$5 — with literature estimates ranging from ~3:1 to ~7:1.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: A 3x-7x flow multiplier means flow-of-funds and forced-rebalancing analysis is more predictive of price than fundamentals.
insights.quote: $1 of net buying can increase aggregate market capitalization by approximately $5.

insights.claim: Inelasticity ensures the trends the feedback architecture produces are larger than the flows that created them — the move exceeds the cause.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: Reinforces convexity in trend payoffs: the structural amplification favors persistent directional positioning.
insights.quote: The inner elasticity ensures that the trends that produces are larger than the flows that created them.

themes.theme: Agent-based-model evidence and the 25% phase transition

insights.claim: In a minimal two-agent model (price-conditioned 'chartists' vs value-based 'fundamentalists'), 0% chartists reproduces textbook efficient markets: Hurst ~0.54, negative excess kurtosis (-0.2), thinner-than-normal tails, zero five-sigma events.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: Shows the Gaussian/efficient world only exists with zero reinforcing feedback — a world that has never actually existed.
insights.quote: Every assumption of efficient markets held in this world of zero chartists.

insights.claim: At ~20-25% chartists the simulated market undergoes a sudden phase transition: kurtosis erupts, Hurst leaps above 0.85, and fat tails, memory and volatility clustering appear all at once — a modest minority of reinforcing agents flips the whole system.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: Real markets sit far above the 25% threshold (stop-losses, margin calls, vol-targeting, options hedging are all feedback), so the fat-tailed regime is the permanent state.
insights.quote: we only needed to put 25% chartists who are price sensitive working in the reinforcing direction to create this phase transition

insights.claim: The feedback-on simulation became statistically indistinguishable from 68 real futures markets spanning 8 asset classes, 6 continents and 41 years — feedback alone, with no news, fundamentals, central banks or geopolitics, reproduces the real-market fingerprint.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: Strong (causal-experiment-grade) evidence that internal feedback, not macro narrative, is the central driver of market statistics.
insights.quote: feedback is sufficient to generate the statistical signatures we observe

insights.claim: Across 1985-2025 rolling 5-year windows, the Hurst exponent never fell to the 0.5 random-walk baseline, fluctuating between 0.62 and 0.82 and surviving Black Monday, electronic trading, the GFC, HFT and the COVID crash.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: Trend persistence is a continuous 40-year structural feature, not a fading anomaly — supporting strategic, not tactical, allocation to trend.
insights.quote: the Hurst exponent never once fell to the random walk baseline of 0.5. It fluctuated between 0.62 and 0.82

insights.claim: Trend following is not exploiting a temporary inefficiency that might close; it is harvesting a 40-year structural feature, and with passive diluting balancing agents the architecture is hardening — same fingerprint with more violence per unit of flow than 20 years ago.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: Core bullish case for the CTA/trend space: the edge is structural and strengthening, not decaying.
insights.quote: the architecture is not weakening, it's hardening

insights.claim: The world's 68 futures markets behave as one permanently coupled feedback engine, not 68 independent systems — the fraction sharing the majority feedback sign averaged ~0.6 (vs ~0.55 if independent) and reached 0.82 in the most coordinated regimes.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: Cross-asset diversification is weaker than it appears; correlated systematic models can synchronize drawdowns and crowd the same trades.
insights.quote: They are one permanently coupled feedback engine with different surface manifestations in different asset classes

insights.claim: The cross-market coupling is caused by practitioners deploying the same correlated models across many of the same markets, so a buy signal in one market tends to coincide with buy signals in others.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: Crowding risk: the diversification trend followers rely on is itself the source of synchronized, correlated positioning.
insights.quote: Many of the same investors apply the same models across many of the same markets. That's what's creating the coupling.

insights.claim: The coupling tilts toward positive feedback (trends emerge) during crisis episodes and toward negative feedback (oscillation) during QE-suppressed regimes, but the coupling itself never disappears.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: QE/liquidity-suppression regimes are the structural headwind for trend; crisis regimes the tailwind — watch policy stance as a regime signal.
insights.quote: during quantitative easing suppressed regimes, the system collectively tilts towards negative feedback and oscillation emerges

insights.claim: Loss of ecological diversity is dangerous: when strategies concentrate into a calm ('calm/car') regime and lever up via structured products to extract return from low vol, the system builds hidden risk and becomes fragile.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: Periods of strategy homogeneity plus leverage are the setup for non-linear breakouts — monitor crowding and embedded leverage in low-vol regimes.
insights.quote: the market is actually building up risk in that car environment because it's getting a concentration of strategy in it

insights.claim: A small deviation out of an over-concentrated, levered calm regime produces non-linear amplification and a massive breakout because the suppressed risk uncoils all at once.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: Suppressed-vol regimes are not safe; they are coiled springs — favors long-convexity/tail and trend positioning into the breakout.
insights.quote: you suddenly get this non-linear amplification and and this massive breakout that occurs out of that because the risk was building up

insights.claim: The crowd into passive sets up a theoretical 'extinction-level event' — a massive uncoiling when everyone-passive meets a regime explosion — consistent with how complex adaptive markets cycle through growth-growth-extinction phases.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: Flags a fat-tail crash scenario embedded in passive dominance — a reason to hold crash protection against an index unwind.
insights.quote: What happens when suddenly they're they're met with this this explosion out of of a regime or a behavior that satisfies. You suddenly get this massive uncoiling

insights.claim: Multi-strat 'pod shops' grew by trading against trend followers and suppressing breakouts, but when markets do break out they end up on the wrong side and amplify the eventual move.
insights.speaker: host (Niels Kaastrup-Larsen)
insights.so_what: The convergent flows that cap volatility today can become accelerants in a breakout — second-order fuel for large trends.
insights.quote: when the markets then do break out, they find themselves on the wrong side of that trade and then that actually amplifies the the the outcome

insights.claim: Demography is the main structural force working against passive; the headwind and the eventual tailwind from a passive unwind may fall on different generations of investors.
insights.speaker: host (Niels Kaastrup-Larsen)
insights.so_what: Frames passive dominance as cyclical over a generational horizon — relevant for very long-horizon allocators expecting a reversal.
insights.quote: what works against passive it's to some degree is demography

themes.theme: Risk management: variance as control, correlation as diagnostic

insights.claim: A trend-following engine runs on variance, not correlation: positions are sized by Average True Range (ATR), so when an instrument's variance rises, position size shrinks automatically with no discretion before the next bar prints.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: Counters the critique that trend ignores single-instrument stress — variance-based sizing is the built-in within-instrument risk control.
insights.quote: every position, for example, in my program is sized by average true range, ATR, which is a direct measure of the instrument's own variance

insights.claim: Correlation is the diagnostic (it tells you what the system is doing across instruments) and variance is the control (it tells you how to size, when to cut, where to back off) — a trend follower needs both at different levels of the architecture.
insights.speaker: Richard Brennan
insights.so_what: A clean mental model for separating portfolio-level diagnosis from position-level risk control.
insights.quote: The system tells you the weather and variance tells you whether to put on another coat right now.

themes.theme: Market snapshot and CTA positioning, May 2026

insights.claim: SocGen's spring 2026 investor sentiment survey shows discretionary global macro most in demand at 59%, with CTA trend far down at 29% interest and CTA non-trend at 28%; multi-strategy systematic fell to its lowest ranking of the last five surveys.
insights.speaker: host (Niels Kaastrup-Larsen)
insights.so_what: Low survey interest in trend can be a contrarian positive given the structural case; declining demand for multi-strat signals waning pod-shop popularity.
insights.quote: with 59% respondents, it's discretionary global macro

insights.claim: Through May 2026 trend was modestly positive: SocGen CTA index +0.72% in May / +11% YTD, SocGen Trend +1.07% / +11.31% YTD, while MSCI World +3.8% in May, the S&P 500 +4.43% (~+10.38% YTD, near all-time highs) and the US aggregate bond index roughly flat.
insights.speaker: host (Niels Kaastrup-Larsen)
insights.so_what: Equities outpacing trend in May with bonds flat frames the relative-performance backdrop for systematic allocators.
insights.quote: SocGen trend up 1.07 up 11.31 percent

insights.claim: The TTU trend barometer fell to 39% at month-end (a level untouched for a while), and oil was down more than 5% over the prior week, signaling near-term weakening in the trend environment.
insights.speaker: host (Niels Kaastrup-Larsen)
insights.so_what: A falling short-term trend barometer is a leading indicator of choppier conditions ahead for trend strategies.
insights.quote: trend barometer update it doesn't look good at coming to the end of the month. It's down to 39%
